projects.name: Solar + Energy Storage on Garage
projects.description: Install 5-7 solar panels on south-facing garage roof face (20x12 ft, ~2-2.8 kW) plus battery storage. Solar = standalone ROI investment (~5yr payback at $0.42/kWh). Battery = generator alternative (no lot space for generator). 30% federal ITC applies to both when paired.
projects.notes: DESIGN: South garage face only. 5 portrait panels = 2.0 kW; 7 mixed = 2.8 kW. ~3,200-4,500 kWh/yr production (20-26% offset). All-in after ITC: solar ~$9-10k, battery ~$9-10k. VENDORS: Sunergy (sunergy.com) — EnergySage 2026 CA Installer of Year, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door. NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com) — Diamond Certified, 35yr, San Mateo County, 40yr warranty. Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy) — online only. AVOID: Sunrun.

tasks.title: Analyze energy usage and size system
tasks.notes: 17,178 kWh/yr at $0.42/kWh avg. Target: 5-7 panels south garage face (20x12 ft) = 2-2.8 kW. ~5yr payback after ITC.

tasks.title: Research and shortlist solar installers
tasks.notes: Shortlist: Sunergy (sunergy.com, Enphase Platinum, no door-to-door), NRG Clean Power (nrgcleanpower.com, Diamond Certified, San Mateo County), Tesla Energy (tesla.com/energy, online only). Avoid Sunrun.

tasks.title: Contact installers for quotes
tasks.notes: Spec: south garage face (20x12 ft, 5-7 panels, ~2-2.8 kW), paired battery (Enphase IQ or Powerwall 3), detached garage sub-panel interconnect. Ask each for: $/W, panel brand, inverter type, battery spec, warranty, timeline, ITC total. Request free shade/LIDAR analysis.

tasks.title: Compare quotes and select installer
tasks.notes: Prefer Enphase microinverters over string inverter. Compare $/W, battery spec, warranty, timeline. 30% ITC on full system incl. battery.

tasks.title: Confirm ITC timing — credit files in year PTO is granted
tasks.notes: 30% federal ITC on Form 5695. Claimed in tax year Permission to Operate granted by PG&E. Battery qualifies only when solar-paired. Plan install timing accordingly.

observations.summary: The audio contains intermittent, high-frequency sounds that are not clearly identifiable as a specific event like a bark or alarm in the backyard setting. — The high spectral centroid and high high-band energy suggest a sharp, possibly electronic or impact-related sound, but the overall pattern is too sporadic for a confident classification.

The audio contains intermittent, moderate-level sounds consistent with general outdoor activity, but no distinct event is clearly identifiable. — The energy distribution is spread across mid and high bands, and the spectral centroid is moderate, suggesting general outdoor noise rather than a specific alarm or speech event.

The audio appears to contain intermittent sounds consistent with dog barking in the dog run area. — The high mid-band energy (0.621) and the rule guess pointing towards speech/bark, combined with the visual transients in the spectrogram, strongly suggest barking from the Australian Shepherds.
The living room is visually empty and still, but audio indicates activity in the backyard with dogs barking and sharp, alarm-like sounds.

The audio appears to be mostly quiet with some low-level, intermittent sounds, consistent with background noise in the living room. — The low energy levels and the rule guess of 'tv' with moderate confidence suggest background ambient noise, but the overall spectral profile doesn't strongly match any specific event like a bark or alarm.

The audio contains intermittent, high-frequency sounds consistent with potential alarms or sharp transients in the backyard. — The high spectral centroid (3975 Hz) and high high-band energy (0.783) suggest sharp, high-pitched sounds, but the overall pattern is not definitively characteristic of a bark or alarm, leading to an unknown classification.

The audio contains intermittent, high-frequency sounds that are not clearly identifiable as a specific event in the backyard. — The high spectral centroid and high high-band energy suggest a sharp, possibly electronic or impact-related sound, but the low overall energy and lack of sustained pattern make definitive classification difficult.

The audio contains distinct, intermittent bursts of sound consistent with dog barking in the dog run area. — The high peak/RMS ratio (5.80) and the energy distribution across mid-band frequencies strongly suggest vocalization, fitting the profile for barks, especially given the presence of two dogs.

The audio suggests an alarm sound was detected in the backyard area. — The high spectral centroid (4033 Hz) and high high-band energy (0.828) strongly point towards an alarm or sharp transient, aligning with the rule guess.

The audio contains distinct sounds consistent with dogs barking in the dog run area. — The high mid-band energy and the rule guess strongly suggest barking, which is expected given the presence of two Australian Shepherd dogs.

The audio contains intermittent, sharp sounds consistent with outdoor activity, but no clear signature matches the known categories. — The high peak/RMS ratio (4.82) suggests transients, and the high high-band energy (0.819) points towards sharp, high-frequency sounds, but the overall pattern is too ambiguous for a definitive classification.

The audio contains intermittent, broadband sounds consistent with general outdoor activity, but no specific event like a bark or alarm is clearly dominant. — The high mid-band and high-band energy suggest some activity, but the low energy levels and low spectral centroid make definitive classification difficult. The rule guess points to 'alarm' but the overall energy profile is low.
The audio appears to contain intermittent, low-energy sounds consistent with general outdoor ambient noise in the dog run area.
The side passage and backyard are empty, and the white gate remains closed. While the audio suggests some dog barking or transient sounds, no people or animals are visible in the camera's view.

The audio contains intermittent, sharp sounds consistent with an impact or transient noise in the front yard. — The high peak/RMS ratio (6.41) and the rule guess of 'impact' suggest a sudden, non-rhythmic sound, but the spectral features do not strongly point to a specific known event like a bark or alarm.

The audio appears to contain intermittent bursts of sound consistent with dog barking in the dog run area. — The mid-band energy is relatively high, and the peak/RMS ratio suggests transient sounds, which aligns with barking. The spectral centroid is also within the expected range for barks/speech.

The audio contains intermittent, high-frequency sounds that are not clearly identifiable as a specific event in the backyard. — The high spectral centroid and high high-band energy suggest a sharp, possibly electronic or impact-related sound, but the overall low energy and lack of sustained pattern make definitive classification difficult.

The audio appears to contain low-level, intermittent background noise, possibly related to ambient household sounds in the living room. — The low energy levels and the rule guess of 'music' with moderate confidence suggest non-specific background sound, but no distinct event like a bark, alarm, or doorbell is clearly present.

The audio appears to contain sounds consistent with dog barking in the dog run area. — The high mid-band energy (0.334) and the peak/RMS ratio (4.81) are suggestive of barks, which aligns with the presence of two Australian Shepherd dogs.
The living room is empty and visually still, but audio indicates intermittent barking and sharp sounds coming from the backyard, suggesting activity in the dog run area.

insights.summary: [living_room] An alarm sound was detected in the backyard area, coinciding with dog barking.
[living_room] Activity detected in the backyard via audio, though the living room remains empty.
Consistent evening energy spike: avg peak 4.19 kWh/hr typically around 20:00 PDT across 13 days. Likely AC/HVAC cooling cycle. At $0.35/kWh this peak window costs ~$8.80/evening.
Energy spike on Wed May 13 at 09AM PDT: 4.41 kWh vs baseline 1.94 kWh (2.3x normal).
[doorbell] The front door camera is offline, which prevents visual monitoring of the front entrance.

There is a persistent discrepancy between the audio sensors and the camera feeds: high-frequency, alarm-like sounds are repeatedly detected in the backyard and living room (12:30, 12:32), but the cameras confirm the areas are empty and quiet. This suggests a potential technical fault or a non-visible external stimulus (like wildlife or distant construction) that is triggering the alarm system, which needs immediate investigation.
Dogs barking repeatedly at entryway (3x since 11:17AM).
Dogs barking repeatedly at living room (2x since 11:26AM).
Dogs barking repeatedly at dog run (2x since 11:45AM).
[living_room] Audio suggests a potential alarm or barking in the backyard, though no activity is visible in the living room.
